devotional.title: Unity in Diversity
devotional.content:
{AtlasWarmF}{or}
just as each of us has one body with many members, and these members 
do not all have the same function, so in Christ we, though many, form 
one body, and each member belongs to all the others.

A system of equations is a beautiful model for community. Each equation 
brings its own constraint, its own perspective on truth. No single 
equation can find the solution alone. But together—in the place where 
their truths intersect—they find what none could find individually.

Consider what we've learned: substitution lets one equation speak 
through another; elimination removes barriers to see clearly; three 
dimensions require three perspectives. These mathematical methods 
mirror spiritual truths about how we work together.
devotional.reflection:
How does your community of faith function like a 
system of equations? What unique "constraints" or perspectives do you 
bring that help the community find solutions?

epigraph.text:
For where two or three gather in my name, there am I 
with them.

items:
A **system of linear equations** consists of two or more 
 linear equations with the same variables

**Graphing method:** Graph both equations; solution is the 
 intersection point

**Substitution method:** Solve one equation for a variable, 
 substitute into the other

**Elimination method:** Add/subtract equations to eliminate 
 a variable

**Three-variable systems:** Reduce to a $2 \times 2$ system 
 by eliminating one variable

rows: One equation already solved for a variable
Both equations in standard form; nice coefficients
